serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw soybean sprouts is approximately 70 g (about 1 cup), commonly used as a vegetable side dish or stir-fry ingredient in Asian cuisine.

nutrient_source.title: Soybean sprouts, raw — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for soybean sprouts, raw (CR ID: F14040059). All core macros and available micronutrients are exact matches from this approved primary source. Saturated fat, sugar, and vitamin C are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) as the HPB record does not disaggregate these values; adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — soybean sprouts analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 18 for raw soybean sprouts is consistent with published values for legume sprouts in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Soybean sprouts are a low-GI food due to high protein content (13.1 g/100g) slowing glucose absorption. GL per serving calculated as (18 × 9.57 × 0.70) / 100 = 1.2 per 70 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (14 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (484 mg/100g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Raw soybean sprouts are highly suitable for hypertension management due to their very low sodium content (14 mg/100g) and high potassium (484 mg/100g); consume without added salt or soy sauce to preserve the beneficial sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 18) and high protein content (13.1 g/100g) attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 18 and 13.1 g protein per 100g, raw soybean sprouts produce a minimal postprandial glucose rise and are suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management; avoid cooking with sweet sauces or oyster sauce that add rapidly absorbed sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.0 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (484 mg/100g), and soy isoflavones with cardioprotective antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw soybean sprouts support cardiovascular health through their low saturated fat content, zero dietary cholesterol, high potassium for vascular tone, and soy isoflavones that may reduce LDL oxidation; consume raw or lightly steamed to maximise isoflavone and potassium bioavailability.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of strong anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3, polyphenols above threshold) despite low saturated fat and presence of isoflavones and vitamin C
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soybean sprouts are not contraindicated in rheumatoid arthritis and provide vitamin C (15 mg/100g) and isoflavones with antioxidant properties, but lack the omega-3 fatty acids or concentrated polyphenols required to qualify as a positive anti-inflammatory food; consume lightly cooked to preserve vitamin C.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw soybean sprouts (Glycine max) are the germinated shoots of soybeans, characterised by thick white stems topped with pale yellow cotyledons. They are consumed raw or lightly cooked across East and Southeast Asian cuisines, including Chinese, Korean, and Singaporean cooking, where they appear in stir-fries, soups, salads, and as a garnish for noodle dishes. Their texture is crisp and crunchy when raw, becoming tender with brief cooking, and their flavour is mild and slightly nutty. Nutritionally, raw soybean sprouts are notably high in protein at 13.1 g per 100g — exceptional for a vegetable — and provide 6.7 g fat, 9.6 g carbohydrate, and 122 kcal per 100g. They are very low in sodium (14 mg/100g) and rich in potassium (484 mg/100g), making them a strong DASH-diet food for blood pressure management. Their glycemic index is low (GI 18), supporting glycaemic stability in Type 2 Diabetes. Calcium (67 mg/100g), iron (2.1 mg/100g), and vitamin C (approximately 15 mg/100g) contribute to bone health, oxygen transport, and antioxidant defence. Soy isoflavones present in the cotyledons provide additional antioxidant and potential cardioprotective effects. Raw consumption maximises potassium and vitamin C availability. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and are suitable for plant-based, low-sodium, and high-protein dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Are soybean sprouts good for diabetes?
Are soybean sprouts high in protein?
Can I eat soybean sprouts raw?
What is the GI of soybean sprouts?
Are soybean sprouts good for blood pressure?
How many calories in soybean sprouts?
What is the difference between mung bean sprouts and soybean sprouts?
Are soybean sprouts good for heart health?
How much potassium in soybean sprouts?

notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: fatty acid fractionation data not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record (CR ID: F14040059); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) was consulted as a cross-reference but not adopted as primary source to preserve HPB provenance. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: same basis as monounsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid fractionation not reported in HPB Foodo for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted but not adopted as primary. trans_fat_g set to -1: trans fat content not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; raw plant foods are expected to contain negligible trans fat but no explicit value is available from the primary source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: vitamin A (RAE) not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg set to -1: thiamine not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg set to -1: riboflavin not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. vitamin_b12_mcg set to -1: vitamin B12 not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; plant foods do not naturally contain B12 so confirmed absence is expected but no explicit zero is available from the primary source. folate_mcg_dfe set to -1: folate (DFE) not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: vitamin D not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; raw plant foods contain negligible vitamin D but no explicit value is available from the primary source. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: vitamin E not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. phosphorus_mg set to -1: phosphorus not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. magnesium_mg set to -1: magnesium not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. zinc_mg set to -1: zinc not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. selenium_mcg set to -1: selenium not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) consulted as cross-reference only. saturated_fat_g (1.0 g) and sugar_g (3.0 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452, Soy bean sprouts, raw) as the HPB record does not disaggregate these values; documented as adapted values. vitamin_c_mg (15.0 mg) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11452) as a cross-reference value not present in the HPB record. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source — soybean sprouts are a plant food containing no dietary cholesterol. GI of 18 is an analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for legume sprouts and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). GL per serving calculated as (18 × 9.57 × 0.70) / 100 = 1.2 per 70 g serving. This food is classified as a generic raw vegetable ingredient; no Singaporean dish-specific multilingual requirements apply beyond the Pan-Asian context, but Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names have been provided given the food's prevalence in Singapore cuisine.
